ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clearly stated new neural architecture (Similarity Encoder) that directly learns a mapping to low-dimensional embeddings by training a bottleneck network to reconstruct rows of an arbitrary similarity matrix (Sec.2, eqns and architecture), and explicitly contrasts this with spectral methods (kPCA/isomap) while claiming advantages for out-of-sample mapping and unknown/human-derived similarities (Sec.2.1–2.2). The Context Encoder further reframes CBOW/negative sampling as a special case of this similarity-preserving view and uses averaged local/global context vectors to produce OOV and sense-sensitive embeddings (Sec.3), which is a notable extension of word2vec. Overall the techniques are a meaningful, non-trivial combination and extension of existing ideas (NN bottleneck + similarity reconstruction) with clear differentiation from prior spectral and word-embedding methods, yielding a contribution that is more than purely incremental but not a radical departure. | B(4): The paper proposes a distinct neural architecture (SimEc) to learn explicit parametric mappings that preserve arbitrary pairwise similarities—addressing kPCA’s scalability and out-of-sample limitations by training a bottleneck NN to approximate S′=YW−1 (Sec.2 and 2.1). Its framing of word2vec as an instance of similarity-encoding and the resulting Context Encoder (ConEc) that constructs OOV and sense-sensitive embeddings via local/global context vectors is a clear, practical extension (Sec.3). While the overall idea builds on well-studied spectral methods and embedding literature, the specific combination (NN bottleneck trained to reproduce arbitrary similarity matrices) and the ConEc interpretation/usage represent a notable, non-trivial contribution that is clearly differentiated from prior work in the text.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a clearly stated new neural architecture (Similarity Encoder) that directly learns a mapping to low-dimensional embeddings by training a bottleneck network to reconstruct rows of an arbitrary similarity matrix (Sec.2, eqns and architecture), and explicitly contrasts this with spectral methods (kPCA/isomap) while claiming advantages for out-of-sample mapping and unknown/human-derived similarities (Sec.2.1–2.2). The Context Encoder further reframes CBOW/negative sampling as a special case of this similarity-preserving view and uses averaged local/global context vectors to produce OOV and sense-sensitive embeddings (Sec.3), which is a notable extension of word2vec. Overall the techniques are a meaningful, non-trivial combination and extension of existing ideas (NN bottleneck + similarity reconstruction) with clear differentiation from prior spectral and word-embedding methods, yielding a contribution that is more than purely incremental but not a radical departure.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a distinct neural architecture (SimEc) to learn explicit parametric mappings that preserve arbitrary pairwise similarities—addressing kPCA’s scalability and out-of-sample limitations by training a bottleneck NN to approximate S′=YW−1 (Sec.2 and 2.1). Its framing of word2vec as an instance of similarity-encoding and the resulting Context Encoder (ConEc) that constructs OOV and sense-sensitive embeddings via local/global context vectors is a clear, practical extension (Sec.3). While the overall idea builds on well-studied spectral methods and embedding literature, the specific combination (NN bottleneck trained to reproduce arbitrary similarity matrices) and the ConEc interpretation/usage represent a notable, non-trivial contribution that is clearly differentiated from prior work in the text.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow (Introduction → SimEc method → experiments → ConEc → experiments → Conclusion), and the main contributions (SimEc and ConEc) are stated early in the introduction. Notation is mostly consistent and defined (e.g. xi ∈ R^D, yi ∈ R^d, W matrices and S ∈ R^{N×N}), though the use of σ−1 for the output nonlinearity is slightly unconventional and could confuse readers. Figures and empirical examples (MNIST, 20 newsgroups, CoNLL NER) are appropriate and referenced, but the text provides few concrete numeric results in-line and several figure/table references make their informativeness unclear in the provided excerpt. Overall the writing is clear, but there are minor presentation issues: some duplicated/repeated sentences, LaTeX artifacts and citation inconsistencies near the end, and a few stylistic/grammatical blemishes. | B(3): Overall the paper is well-organized (Intro → methods (SimEc/ConEc) → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (introducing SimEc and ConEc in the Introduction). Notation is mostly defined (e.g. xi ∈ R^D, yi ∈ R^d, W matrices) but there are occasional ambiguities and formatting glitches that hurt clarity (the use of σ−1 as an output nonlinearity is potentially confusing, and the regularization term appears garbled: “λ 1 d2 − d ∥∥W−1W>−1 − diag(...)∥∥1”). Figures/tables are frequently referenced (Figures 1–9, Appendix) but are not visible here and some figure references/descriptions would benefit from being more self-contained. The manuscript suffers from repeated lines, LaTeX/rendering artifacts and minor grammatical/typographic issues (e.g. title fragments, duplicated citation lines), which require cleanup to improve readability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow (Introduction → SimEc method → experiments → ConEc → experiments → Conclusion), and the main contributions (SimEc and ConEc) are stated early in the introduction. Notation is mostly consistent and defined (e.g. xi ∈ R^D, yi ∈ R^d, W matrices and S ∈ R^{N×N}), though the use of σ−1 for the output nonlinearity is slightly unconventional and could confuse readers. Figures and empirical examples (MNIST, 20 newsgroups, CoNLL NER) are appropriate and referenced, but the text provides few concrete numeric results in-line and several figure/table references make their informativeness unclear in the provided excerpt. Overall the writing is clear, but there are minor presentation issues: some duplicated/repeated sentences, LaTeX artifacts and citation inconsistencies near the end, and a few stylistic/grammatical blemishes.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well-organized (Intro → methods (SimEc/ConEc) → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (introducing SimEc and ConEc in the Introduction). Notation is mostly defined (e.g. xi ∈ R^D, yi ∈ R^d, W matrices) but there are occasional ambiguities and formatting glitches that hurt clarity (the use of σ−1 as an output nonlinearity is potentially confusing, and the regularization term appears garbled: “λ 1 d2 − d ∥∥W−1W>−1 − diag(...)∥∥1”). Figures/tables are frequently referenced (Figures 1–9, Appendix) but are not visible here and some figure references/descriptions would benefit from being more self-contained. The manuscript suffers from repeated lines, LaTeX/rendering artifacts and minor grammatical/typographic issues (e.g. title fragments, duplicated citation lines), which require cleanup to improve readability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper's main claims (SimEc can approximate spectral embeddings and provide an explicit out-of-sample mapping; ConEc can produce context-sensitive and OOV word embeddings) are clearly stated and supported by plausible derivations (e.g. S' = Y Y^T, linear SimEc Y = XW0, S' = YW_{-1}) and qualitative experiments on MNIST, 20 Newsgroups and CoNLL NER. The mathematical presentation is conceptually correct (including the orthogonality regularizer on W_{-1}), but no formal guarantees are given that gradient-based training recovers the kPCA eigen-solution and some identifiability/normalization issues are not addressed. Experimental methodology is reasonable but thin: comparisons are mostly qualitative, quantitative results and statistical tests are sparse or omitted (NER results referenced but not fully reported), and stronger baselines/ablation would be needed to fully substantiate the claims. | B(3): The paper states clear technical claims (Sec.2: SimEc architecture and objectives; Sec.2.1: relation to kPCA) and gives plausible constructions (MSE objective, orthogonality regularizer, and the linear W0/W−1 interpretation) and empirical demonstrations (MNIST, 20 Newsgroups, CoNLL NER, Sec.2.3 and 3.1). However, mathematical arguments are informal (no convergence or identifiability proofs that W−1→Y^T or that the NN will recover kPCA/isomap solutions beyond intuitive algebra), and key experimental details and quantitative comparisons/statistical tests are missing (results are described qualitatively and via figures but no thorough baselines or significance analysis). Overall the methods are technically plausible and supported by experiments at a preliminary level, but the paper lacks complete theoretical justification and rigorous experimental validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims (SimEc can approximate spectral embeddings and provide an explicit out-of-sample mapping; ConEc can produce context-sensitive and OOV word embeddings) are clearly stated and supported by plausible derivations (e.g. S' = Y Y^T, linear SimEc Y = XW0, S' = YW_{-1}) and qualitative experiments on MNIST, 20 Newsgroups and CoNLL NER. The mathematical presentation is conceptually correct (including the orthogonality regularizer on W_{-1}), but no formal guarantees are given that gradient-based training recovers the kPCA eigen-solution and some identifiability/normalization issues are not addressed. Experimental methodology is reasonable but thin: comparisons are mostly qualitative, quantitative results and statistical tests are sparse or omitted (NER results referenced but not fully reported), and stronger baselines/ablation would be needed to fully substantiate the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear technical claims (Sec.2: SimEc architecture and objectives; Sec.2.1: relation to kPCA) and gives plausible constructions (MSE objective, orthogonality regularizer, and the linear W0/W−1 interpretation) and empirical demonstrations (MNIST, 20 Newsgroups, CoNLL NER, Sec.2.3 and 3.1). However, mathematical arguments are informal (no convergence or identifiability proofs that W−1→Y^T or that the NN will recover kPCA/isomap solutions beyond intuitive algebra), and key experimental details and quantitative comparisons/statistical tests are missing (results are described qualitatively and via figures but no thorough baselines or significance analysis). Overall the methods are technically plausible and supported by experiments at a preliminary level, but the paper lacks complete theoretical justification and rigorous experimental validation.
